title: Bugbot is now over 3x faster, 22% cheaper, and finds 10% more bugs
text: Cursor reports Bugbot is over 3x faster, 22% cheaper, finds 10% more bugs per review, and 90% of runs finish in under three minutes. The post also introduces /review and incremental review for catching issues before push.

title: Bugbot setup and branch-protection options
text: Bugbot runs on every PR update or manually from a comment. The docs cover repo enablement, branch protection with the Cursor Bugbot check, and review analytics APIs for measuring findings and cost.

title: Semantic search improves agent performance
text: Cursor says semantic search plus grep yields 12.5% higher codebase QA accuracy on average, 0.3% higher code retention overall, 2.6% higher retention on repos with 1,000+ files, and fewer dissatisfied follow-up requests.

title: Indexing and search configuration in Cursor
text: Cursor indexes a workspace automatically, enables semantic search at about 80% completion, syncs every five minutes, and lets users tune quality through .cursorignore and Settings > Indexing.

title: Cursor Automations operational examples
text: Cursor says Bugbot-like automations run thousands of times a day and have caught millions of bugs. Internal security review has caught multiple vulnerabilities and critical bugs, and incident-response automations cut response time significantly.

title: Automation triggers and tool configuration
text: The docs spell out trigger selection, repo scope, and tool grants for PR comments, Slack, memories, MCP, and more, making the workflow reproducible for review, monitoring, and cron use cases.

title: Codex operational improvements
text: OpenAI says GPT-5-Codex can work for more than seven hours on complex tasks, cached cloud tasks cut median completion time by 90%, and bottom-decile turns use 93.7% fewer tokens than GPT-5. It also reviews the vast majority of internal PRs and catches hundreds of issues per day.

title: Codex CLI quickstart
text: The README gives install commands for Mac, Linux, Windows, npm, Homebrew, and release binaries, then tells users to run codex and sign in with ChatGPT.

title: Prompt changes can regress coding quality
text: Anthropic traced recent Claude Code quality issues to multiple product-layer changes. One prompt-line change caused a 3% drop on both Opus 4.6 and 4.7 in a broader eval set, leading to an immediate revert and new requirements for broader evals, ablations, soak periods, and gradual rollouts.

title: Capability evals should graduate into regression suites
text: Anthropic recommends 20 to 50 real tasks, clear separation between capability and regression evals, deterministic graders where possible, and continuous regression suites once an ability has stabilized. It also notes that fixing eval or scaffold bugs can move benchmark results dramatically, such as 42% to 95% on CORE-Bench.

title: Containment patterns for agent products
text: Anthropic argues for environment-layer containment first. Its Claude Code sandbox reduced permission prompts by 84%, auto mode catches about 83% of overeager behaviors, and model-layer defenses alone are not enough to stop prompt-injection-style exfiltration.

title: AI Gateway for production reliability
text: Vercel positions AI Gateway as protection against single-provider outages and rate limits. It says the same system has powered v0 for millions of users and runs on infrastructure delivering sub-20ms latency.

title: Model fallback configuration
text: The docs show how to set a primary model plus ordered fallback models, combine model and provider routing, and inspect modelAttempts and providerAttempts metadata to see exactly what failed over.

title: GPT-5 mini pricing and coding scores
text: OpenAI reports GPT-5 mini scores 71.0% on SWE-bench Verified and 71.6% on Aider polyglot while costing $0.25 per 1M input tokens and $2 per 1M output tokens. GPT-5 itself uses 22% fewer output tokens and 45% fewer tool calls than o3 on SWE-bench Verified.

title: Prompt caching for repeated codebase context
text: Anthropic says prompt caching can reduce cost by up to 90% and latency by up to 85%. Example reductions include 11.5s to 2.4s TTFT on a 100k-token prompt and roughly 10s to 2.5s on a long multi-turn conversation.
